tokens: Lunar Module Antares has come alive per the Flight Plan with Al Shepard and Ed Mitchell at the helm.
The crew's greatest concern at the moment is whether the previously problematic docking system will give them a trouble-free release of the LM from the Command Module mothership.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Editor's note:All transcript times are presented according to the GET update at 054:53:36 that saw the mission timer moved forward 40 minutes, 2.90 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: No, you can go ahead and close your hatch.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: With your meter problem there on the S-band, Ed, that you might change your LOS procedure to go on Pitch, 90; Yaw, 0; and Slew.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: And I just now noticed the meter is back with me again, but I suspect it's not very reliable.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: We had a Program Alarm 1106 noted.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: We saw it here, and you can press on.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Would you verify your hatch is closed and the Dump Valve, Auto.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: I've got the - all 12 latches released and the hatch in.
And I'm proceeding to vent down the tunnel.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: I've got an AGS K factor for you.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: It's plus 00100, plus all zips, plus 00072.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Do you have a - an LOS PAD for me, Freddo?'
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: I'm showing LOS in about 8 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Apollo 14 is nearing the end of its 11th lunar revolution.
We're now in 103 hours 31 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
As Apollo 14 came out from behind the Moon earlier in this revolution the landing crew, Shepard and Mitchell were in the Lunar Module beginning to power up the systems.
They encountered some problem in locking up with the sterrable antenna of the Lunar Module but this was sorted out later.
Apparently there was a malfunction in one of the needles (ir indicators that showed antenna orientation.
In the checkout of the ascent battery, storage batteries, battery 5 was reported by Mitchell to be in the same shape it was in the test late last night prior to the time it went into lunar orbit.

The open circuit voltage of battery 5 was still hanging in at 36.7 volts and again the loads were cycled on this battery and its sister battery number 6 of up to fairly high averages, shared the load quite well when the load was split and the voltage was dropped down to around 29½ or 30 volts when the full load was applied on it alone with battery 6 turned off.
Landing gear was deployed at 102:59 Ground Elapsed Time and the hatch was closed just prior to LOS, the Lunar Module hatch that is, at about 103 hours 17 minutes.
There was brief mention in the program alarm during the time that a load was being sent up by a command into the Lunar Module computer.
It was program alarm 1106 which turns out to be uplink too fast.
In other words the, ail the numbers were being pumped in at too high a rate for the computer to digest.
We have acquisition again on Apollo 14 in the 12th revolution in 45 minutes 28 seconds from now.

Some of the significant items during that revolution will be of course undocking and separation at about 104:27, throttle check for the descent propulsion system, some photography of the undocking of course, which is sort of standard operating procedure, calibration of the landing point designator which is a grid system in the commanders window in the Lunar Module, calibration of the abort guidance system, command service module circularization maneuver at 105 hours 46 minutes.
The LM will maneuver to observe this and photograph it with a 16 millimeter camera, however this will be on the back side, in other words after the next front side pass.
At 103 hours 35 minutes Ground Elapsed Time this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control 104 hours 18 minutes Ground Elapsed Time about a minute out from acquisition of Apollo 14 coming around from behind the Moon on the 12th lunar revolution.

Flight Director Gerry Griffin has asked all the flight controllers in the room to go amber on his status board and his particular console and as they give him the go for undocking they will punch up green.
28 seconds to acquisition, we should be getting an indication before too many more seconds of incoming telemetry.
That's usually the first thing the network advises the flight director that we have telemetry.
We're standing by and should come any moment now.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Like we're standing by for a Go for Sep.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Do you have lockup for Houston?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: We would like High Bit Rate.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Could you try the Servo again?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Let me put you on Omni until we get undocked.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Do you want the - I got the gimbal angles ready for you, if you're ready to copy.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: On that drift check, I got a GET of 103:58:25; Command Module as follows: 007.69, 113.04, 022.88.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Kitty Hawk and Antares , you have a Go for undock.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: And I'd just like to reiterate some words I think you got passed from Bruce earlier.
If the nominal undock does not take place after you get things damped out again, we need 5 seconds of minus-X thrusted by both vehicles.
And Kitty Hawk should call the thrusting on and off.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: We were told 3 before, but - Okay, we want each one a minus 5 seconds while I'm holding the switch.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: And you should call the on and off command.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I'm going to try the nominal first.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Antares, how do you read on VOX?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Houston, I have you locked up on the steerable.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell

tokens: Okay, let me keep you on it now.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Got to keep the - hold off on it until you do.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Give me Biomed, Left; PCM, Hi.
transcript.speaker: Shepard

tokens: Will you be ready to go at 5?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: - - and you're hanging on the end of the probe.
We'll wait until motions damp here.
I'm going to back off from you.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Okay, we had a normal undocking, Houston.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Deadband Min; Verb 77; go to P00.
transcript.speaker: Shepard-LM

tokens: Boy , you look mighty pretty out there.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Yaw right 60, yaw left 60, pitch up 90.
transcript.speaker: Shepard-LM

tokens: We've lost data on you now.
We'd like your - what you ended up with in Noun 83.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: We ended up with 1 - plus 0.1, minus point - minus 0.1, plus 0.1, and 0.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: I have you back on the Omnis.
It doesn't seem to be tracking.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: I'll give you Low bit rate, if you want it.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: You're around - you want me to verify your tracker light?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: We have you and have the camera on, and you look mighty pretty out there.
transcript.speaker: Shepard-LM

tokens: I've been taking a few shots of you there.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: And I got a REV 12 TCA for you.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: I'd like to recheck those gimbal angles with you again, please.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: Let me just give you the Command Module and LM angles again.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: And we'd like to get the steerable again; Pitch, plus 66; Yaw, minus 43.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Be advised, it seems to track for a few minutes, and then breaks lock, and then heads for the stop.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: The last loss there was due to a problem we had on the site on the ground here.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: And, Antares, did you copy the TCA time?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: We'll proceed with the DPS throttle check, if you're ready.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: That'll be all right, and, if you give us P00 and Data, we'll pump you up a Command Module state vector.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: You have it, and we're proceeding with the throttle check.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: Engine is armed, and we go with the Commander's Min, soft stop; Max; back to Min.
Here we go with the LMP.
It's in Min, soft stop; and going to MAX.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: Antares, the throttle check looks good.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: You can proceed on by the program alarm.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Presently under way onboard Antares is descent propulsion system throttle check.
There was considerable jubilation here in the mission control room when the successful undocking was accomplished.
In the room are the backup Apollo 14 crewmen, Gene Cernan, Ron Evans and Joe Engle, and Apollo 15 Commander, Dave Scott.
Others in the room include Apollo program director, Rocco Petrone, Apollo spacecraft manager, Jim McDivitt, and mission director, Chet Lee.
We'll continue to monitor air-ground as the crew of Antares continues with the checkout of the propulsion systems onboard the Lunar Module.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're going over Hipparchus L and Hipparchus C at 8 minutes before the landing site.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: We got Albategnius on the left.
transcript.speaker: Shepard-LM

tokens: We have Hipparchus J on the right as we're going over it.
It looks like it's right below us, about 1000 feet.
This is Muller ahead of us, Muller A on the left, Muller 0 first.
Hipparchus K out to the right.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: Look's like you're right on the line.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Fred, I don't see how we could clear that ridge ahead of us; it sticks up so far.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: Okay, Ptolemaeus A on the left.
transcript.speaker: Shepard-LM

tokens: We look like we're at about - about a foot above it, and Stu's below us.Here's Herschel out - Herschel out to the right.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: And the camera is started, Freddo, as we cross Lalande N and NA.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: Might even get a picture of Stu ahead of us; we're crossing Lalande C.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: Looks just like on the map as we approach the site.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: Do you have about the same picture with respect to the shadows on those ridges up ahead, Ed, as the map?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: They don't seem quite as long as on the map, Freddo.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: We've spotted the general area of the landing site from in this area on in.
We didn't spot Cone Crater on this pass, however.
transcript.speaker: Shepard-LM

tokens: We'll get a look at it a couple passes later.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: And, Freddo, as we come up on Lansberg here, it's -the terminator is running right across it.
And it was a big one.
It really has some - a steep rim -or rather a high rim, very noticeably high rim from here - from here.
And it's right - the terminator goes right through it.
transcript.speaker: Shepard-LM

tokens: Rare signal tuning noises break into the air to ground channel.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You can use the same LOS procedures on the steerable.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: How do you read Kitty Hawk?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Okay, that pass went just as advertised.
I think we've got plenty of marks on it.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: I'm going to be setting up for ranging Simplex B.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: Okay, we're right on the time line.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: We'd like to crank up the High Gain.
Pitch, minus 87; Yaw, plus 281; Manual and Wide so we can get a tape dump.
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: Okay, minus 87, and say again the Yaw?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: No, make that 0.55; mine keep: varying a little bit.
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: My S-Band Antenna circuit breaker has popped twice now.
I think that may be the reason we're breaking lock occasionally.
We got some problems in it
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: Is that the AC or, on your side, the DC one?
transcript.speaker: Haise

tokens: No, that's the DC one on panel 16.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: The steerable antenna requires both DC and AC power from the EPS to drive it, each governed by an independent circuit breaker.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Seems like it might be overheating on us, probably.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: The circuit breaker is holding for the moment.
transcript.speaker: Mitchell-LM

tokens: Did you get the torquing angles?
transcript.speaker: Roosa

tokens: We'd like you to back out of that 52.
We need to look at a bit here.
transcript.speaker: Haise
